coredata.dc:title: Nanostructured carbon heterojunctions for broadband photodetection: Development roadmap, emerging technologies, and future perspectives
coredata.dc:creator: Sarkar, Krishnendu
Kumar, Praveen
coredata.dc:description: Nanostructured carbon (NC) has been at the forefront of modern research as a potential form of optoelectronic material. The versatility in nanostructuring of this single material entails prolific consequences in materials science and heterojunction based numerous device technologies. Conventional semiconductors when heterogeneously integrated with NC unveil efficient routes for various micro/opto-electronic devices including photodetectors (PDs) due to their effective optoelectronic properties and carrier transport kinetics. Further, their broadened responsivity ranges open a completely new direction towards the development of broadband PDs. In this review, we present a detailed description of heterogeneous integration of carbon nanostructures (graphene, reduced graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, carbon quantum dots, graphene quantum dots, etc.) with different semiconducting materials (Si/Ge, III-Vs, II-VIs, TMDC, 2D Materials, Perovskites, and Flexible substrates) for photodetection application. A general idea about the effect of nanostructuring on the photodetection mechanism and their overall performance in conjunction with different materials is discussed. We also comprehensively discuss and compare the PDs performances for the above discussed carbonaceous heterojunctions, which can give a better overview for designing the next-generation PD devices for the futuristic artificial intelligence (AI) technologies.

head.ce:title: Nanostructured carbon heterojunctions for broadband photodetection: Development roadmap, emerging technologies, and future perspectives

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanostructured carbon (NC) has been at the forefront of modern research as a potential form of optoelectronic material. The versatility in nanostructuring of this single material entails prolific consequences in materials science and heterojunction based numerous device technologies. Conventional semiconductors when heterogeneously integrated with NC unveil efficient routes for various micro/opto-electronic devices including photodetectors (PDs) due to their effective optoelectronic properties and carrier transport kinetics. Further, their broadened responsivity ranges open a completely new direction towards the development of broadband PDs. In this review, we present a detailed description of heterogeneous integration of carbon nanostructures (graphene, reduced graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, carbon quantum dots, graphene quantum dots, etc.) with different semiconducting materials (Si/Ge, III-Vs, II-VIs, TMDC, 2D Materials, Perovskites, and Flexible substrates) for photodetection application. A general idea about the effect of nanostructuring on the photodetection mechanism and their overall performance in conjunction with different materials is discussed. We also comprehensively discuss and compare the PDs performances for the above discussed carbonaceous heterojunctions, which can give a better overview for designing the next-generation PD devices for the futuristic artificial intelligence (AI) technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction: developmental roadmap of NC photodetectors
ce:section.ce:para: Optimized material combination for maximal desirable photoresponse is of utmost concern for photodetectors. During the fabrication and integration of optoelectronic devices, attention should be paid to the specific technical features, such as device configuration and morphology, to provide the maximum performance by optimizing the optical and electronic properties of the material. Several schemes have been adopted successfully by the researchers to maximize the optical absorption of materials through nanostructuring [1,2]. Besides, to enhance the electron-hole pair dissociation and carrier transport, vertical heterojunctions and field-effect transistor configurations are attempted [3–5]. To further increase the interaction of incoming excitation with materials over a wide range of wavelength from ultraviolet (UV) to terahertz (THz), improved waveguiding techniques have been employed [6,7]. Although the device configuration heavily impacts its figure of merits, proper combination of photoactive materials is still crucial for improved response and broadband characteristics. A huge range of materials (organic/inorganic) and their combinations (as composites, heterostructures, alloys, etc.) has been investigated so far to understand the materials science behind photodetection and continuing with successive betterments [1,8–10].

The available reviews in literature have discussed the optoelectronic applications of NC emphasizing a limited section of scientific works. Li et al. in their feature article presented a detailed discussion on CQDs and GQDs about their application in optoelectronic and energy devices including light-emitting diodes, solar cells, photodetectors, photocatalysts, batteries and supercapacitors [34]. In this discussion, the authors presented a substantial illustration on the optical properties of CQDs and GQDs explored so far impressively and their device applications; however, in the discussion about their application as photodetectors the authors considered the heterojunction-based devices in a very limited manner. Similarly, Richter et al. in their comprehensive review covered the fundamental optical properties and photodetection application of all carbon allotropes with a focus on room temperature operated devices [35]. They mostly discussed the device applications based on fundamental optical properties with a brief discussion on CNT and graphene heterojunction-based photodetectors. Besides, Geng et al. in their review presented the photodetection applications of only graphene van der Waals heterojunctions with 2D materials [36]. In an elaborative review, Xie et al. discussed the 2D layered materials including all kinds of metal chalcogenides and their photodetection application [37]. However, the special features of those materials in heterojunction with carbon allotropes were not covered in the review. The research on NC in optoelectronics has become so diverse that a complete coverage of available literature in a single review is difficult to bring in. Therefore, in an attempt to fill the gaps between several comprehensive reviews presented so far, we have discussed the photodetection application of conventional elemental (Si/Ge) and compound (III–V/II-VI) semiconductors, perovskites, TMDCs, 2D materials (black phosphorous, hexagonal BN) with all kinds of NCs reported in the literature in this review. We have addressed the advantages and disadvantages of different individual nanostructured carbons and how they are going to impact future research. Thereafter, we have reviewed the merits and demerits of device configuration and material combinations concerning their nanostructure, synthesis mechanism and assembly. The possible future direction of research is also assessed considering global PD market scenario and development in materials science, especially 2D materials and perovskites. Further, we have also discussed the development of carbonaceous heterojunction PDs for flexible and wearable applications. Some novel heterostructures are discussed to understand the direction of future research. The performance of the PDs has been evaluated based on figure-of-merits. Finally, we conclude the review with reasonable perspective on how nanostructured carbon can be utilized in future and how present shortcomings can be overcome.

Carbon in its different nanostructures demonstrate so much versatility in optical properties that is not observed in any other material system discovered so far. Graphene is a 2D crystal with hexagonal honeycomb lattice structure composed of C atoms in sp2 hybridized orbitals [38]. The thickness of single layer graphene is 0.35 nm and the length of C–C bond is 0.142 nm [39]. There are three electrons in the outer shell of C atoms that form strong σ bonds through sp2 hybridization and the fourth electron forms π [40]. As the basic unit of many carbon nanomaterials, graphene can be curled into carbon nanotubes [41], fragmented to graphene quantum dots and [42] 1D graphene nanoribbons [43]. Each of the nanostructures exhibit distinguishable optical properties, which are covered in this section before proceeding to their PD applications.

Graphene is a semiconductor with zero band gap as the conduction and valence band corresponding to two sublattices in the unit cell are connected to k points at the boundary of first Brillouin zone [40]. Including this zero band gap characteristics with massless Dirac fermions as carriers, graphene's unique optoelectronic properties e.g., high carrier mobility, electron-hole symmetry and highly tunable interband and intraband optical transition over wide spectral range from visible to terahertz frequencies, offer immense opportunities for signal transmission, modulation, light detection, and emission. Contrary to conventional metals, the free carrier concentration and associated Fermi level in graphene can be largely modulated via electrostatic gating or chemical doping due to its low intrinsic carrier density and low density of states [44]. Therefore, the optical properties of graphene which strongly depend on the Fermi level position, can be tuned accordingly. Noticeably, restricting the lateral dimensions of graphene structure will result measurable optical bandgap in CNTs, GNRs and CQDs. The disruption of graphene's 2D network of sp2 hybridized bonds is typically exploited as a means of energy gap opening. On the other hand, functionalization of graphene by surface adsorption of atomic oxygen, fluorine and hydrogen has been demonstrated experimentally to impact such modification [45]. Although partially oxidized graphene i.e., reduced graphene oxide (rGO) may offer tunable optical bandgap in the range from 6.5 eV to 0.25 eV, its electrical conductivity is considerably compromised with that achieved in high quality graphene samples obtained from mechanical exfoliation of graphite or synthesized through vapor phase growth [46]. In spite of that, inclusion of rGO has been experimentally established for easing carrier transport to enhance photodetection which we shall discuss in Section 4.2. Besides, rGO exhibits strong optical absorption in UV range, which shifts towards the visible range as the level of reduction is enhanced i.e. more oxygen functional groups are removed [47]. The luminescence intensity from RGO can be modulated through reduction engineering. On the other hand, for fragmented GQDs the optical properties become more interesting due to lateral confinement of charge carriers. GQDs exhibit interesting tunable luminescence features that can be utilized for advanced imaging and sensing [48]. Carbon dots generally reveals photoluminescence in the range of 440–580 nm when excited at 350–400 nm; whereas, most of the reported GQDs exhibit excitation dependent PL features. It is observed in a general trend that upon excitation the fluorescence color is red-shifted with decreased excitation energy with emission of gradually weaker intensity [49]. It is established that GQD emission is generated from two types of energy states, band gap related intrinsic states and surface related extrinsic states. The intrinsic band gap states are created from isolated polyaromatic structures and the extrinsic energy states are created from surface functional groups [50]. Studies have demonstrated that GQDs having different functional groups e.g. –COOH, –OH, epoxy, –NO2 exhibit different luminescence intensity and yield which is quite beneficial for band selective photodetection. The emission characteristics are interlinked to both surface functionalization and sp2 domains and to distinguish individual influence of sp2 domain size and surface/edge functional groups on PL emission, GQDs with uniform size distribution are required. Besides, GQDs demonstrate multi-photon up-conversion photoluminescence which imposes a great promise to GQDs for in vivo imaging applications [51]. Due to multiphoton imaging, GQDs are capable of deep imaging and precise localization of target tissues which can be utilized to replace inorganic semiconductor QDs ensuring better biocompatibility.

Single walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNT) can be either semiconducting or metallic depending on their geometrical characteristics i.e., the chiral angle [52,53]. Their structure and associated optical characteristics can be identified by a pair of integers (n, m) depicting the chiral vector
C →
h
= n
a →
1
+ m
a →
2
which interconnects two crystallographically equivalent sites on a 2D graphene sheet. Interestingly, when CNTs are in n = m armchair configuration, they exhibit gapless metallic characteristics; while for n ≠ m nonarmchair configuration CNTs exhibit narrow-gap semiconducting characteristics. Resonant Raman spectroscopy has been proved to be an effective tool to determine (n, m) for both metallic and semiconducting CNTs. Single walled CNTs demonstrate photoluminescence in the NIR region which are generated from the highly π-conjugated structures with semiconducting crystalline sp2 carbon networks [54]. However, due to photoinduced mobile excitons and their non-radiative relaxation through collision with nanotube edges, the PL efficiency is decreased. An effective approach to enhance luminescence yield has been to introduce local defects of oxygen and sp3 carbon atoms in the tube structure to create emissive doped sites. Interestingly, armchair SWCNTs are metallic in nature; however, their aqueous suspensions with different diameter distributions exhibit different distinct colors in the visible regime. Unlike surface plasmon resonance in noble metal nanoparticles, the distinct color feature in CNT suspensions is derived by a combination of band structure and selection rules for optical transitions. The optical properties become more promising for optoelectronics when CNTs are configured to nanoribbons by unzipping. Graphene nanoribbons exhibit width dependent sizable bandgaps suitable for optoelectronics while maintaining the outstanding transport properties of graphene [55]. Where the parent material graphene for its linear band dispersion exhibits wavelength independent optical absorbance of 2.3 % in visible range, GNRs due to their quasi-one dimensionality reveals characteristic absorption bands related to band gap openings [56]. In this review we shall categorically illustrate how each NC have been employed in PDs considering their optical and carrier transport characteristics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Basic concepts and efficiency measuring parameters of photodetectors
ce:section.ce:para:
The characteristic parameters used to evaluate the performance of a PD include responsivity, detectivity, quantum efficiency, gain, time response, linear dynamic range, and 3 dB bandwidth. Responsivity (R) of a PD is expressed in terms of the generated photocurrent (

is the illuminated area of the detector. However, the total incoming optical power is not completely used up by the PDs as some proportions of photons are reflected back without being absorbed. Such a lost fraction is quantified in terms of quantum efficiency of the PD as defined by the number of electron-hole pairs per incoming photon. Generally, external quantum efficiency considers all photons striking the device, while internal quantum efficiency considers only the photons that are not reflected. In terms of
R λ
and wavelength of light (λ), the quantum efficiency is expressed as [57]

is the gain defined by the number of actually detected electrons per generated electron-hole pair. It directly depends upon the number of photogenerated charge carriers and carrier mobility.

Another parameter that quantifies the performance of a PD is its noise equivalent power (NEP), which is defined as the optical power that provides a signal-to-noise ratio of one. The NEP can be estimated by dividing the device noise current by
R λ
. The inverse of NEP gives detectivity (D), which implies the minimum optical power to be detected by the device. When D is normalized with the detector optical area and bandwidth (Δf), the resulting parameter gives specific detectivity (D*) as an important performance metric of PD systems [57].

The fast-sensing ability of a PD against the optical excitation is quantified by its response time, which is estimated by the rise and fall time between 10 % and 90 % of the maximum photoresponse value. Rise time and fall time normally differ in value due to differences in excitation and charge relaxation kinetics.

The linear dynamic range of a PD describes the light intensity range under which the current response of the PD is linearly proportional to the light intensity and is expressed as [58].

The 3 dB bandwidth is defined as the modulation frequency of the incident light when the responsivity decreases by 3 dB (=0.707 times the low-frequency value) [57].

Graphene is the two-dimensional structure of carbon that has undergone extensive investigations since it has been found to exhibit ultrahigh field-effect mobility in single-layer form [59]. It is a promising optoelectronic material due to its tunable broadband optical absorption characteristics [60,61]. However, its low optical absorption (2.3 %) in visible and infrared regime compromises its application as high responsivity PD [62]. To compensate for the low visible optical absorption and take advantage of high carrier mobility, it has become a well-adopted endeavor to integrate the ultrahigh carrier mobility graphene with optically active materials, mainly semiconductors to develop highly responsive photodetectors. The photoinduced electron-hole pairs are generated in the semiconductor, dissociated at the interface, and transported by graphene to provide photocurrent. Over the past decade, there has been an enormous investigation on graphene-based photodetectors, which is evident from the exponential increase in the number of research publications for a variety of scientific directions as presented in Fig. 3 (a) and (b) respectively.

Ultra-high responsivity, faster response speed and broadband detection features have been the key aspects behind its popularity as a constituent material for photodetection. However, integrating graphene with photoactive semiconductors to achieve photodetection has a drawback of small operational bandwidth. The long ‘carrier lifetime’ of charge carriers from semiconductors and fast ‘carrier transit time’ due to graphene deliver high gain and responsivity; however, that is too at the cost of response time. Researchers have thought of inducing an electric field to separate the electron-hole pairs and compensate the effect of charge traps. Chen et al. in their work used an organic heterostructure of Pentacene and PTCDA as light-absorbing medium interfaced with graphene in back-gated phototransistor configuration [63]. The device exhibited impressively high responsivity of 105 A/W with gate tunable 28 μs of response time. The built-in electric field in organic heterojunction enabled efficient dissociation of photoexcited carrier pair causing higher performance as compared to bulk absorption. The device configuration is simple yet interesting and is built on the idea that a semiconductor heterojunction will assist in electron-hole pair generation and their separation while the graphene will assist their collection and transport. Definitely, a type II heterojunction is expected with the semiconductors. The semiconductor in contact with graphene should also be band-aligned with graphene. The PD in Ref. [63] fulfills all these conditions; however, the device responsivity does not modulate with gate bias, the response time has been improved with gate bias instead. The gate bias controls the electron population in graphene which in turn controls the recombination of holes trapped in Pentacene as demonstrated in ESI Figure S1. The device design is promising and can be implemented with other semiconductor type II heterojunctions. However, the absence of gate-tunability on responsivity indicates the absence of photogating effect. To achieve this photogating effect and in-turn gate-tunable responsivity, graphene needs to maintain type I band alignment with the adjoining semiconductor to trap electrons. In that case, responsivity may be improved with gate bias.

In a different strategy, Ni et al. used heavily boron-doped colloidal Si QDs with graphene to achieve a broadband response from ultraviolet (UV) to mid-infrared (MIR) [64]. The schematic device configuration is demonstrated in Fig. 4 (a and b). The idea is similar to integrating graphene with semiconducting nanostructures to avail photodetection. The interesting thing is that as the photoactive species the authors have used Si quantum dots heavily doped with boron which is more beneficial than low bandgap HgTe, PbS considering toxicity and achieved broadband absorption from UV to mid-IR. Optical absorption in UV–Vis–NIR regime is attained from band-to-band and sub-band transitions in SiQD while absorption in MIR is achieved from the localized surface plasmon resonance generated from the oscillations of induced holes in Si. The mean size of the used Si QDs is 6 nm and 40 % boron doping is done. Different sized Si QDs are expected to give different optical absorption spectra. Fig. 4(c) presents the variation of photocurrent density as a function of drain-source bias at VGS = 0 V under illumination from UV to MIR. The device is responsive under the broad illumination range and photocurrent density is achieved depending on illumination density. From the responsivity characteristics in Fig. 4(d), highest R in UV–Vis–NIR range is observed at 532 nm excitation while highest R at MIR is obtained at 3.0 μm. The dependence of photoconductive gain on excitation wavelength is demonstrated in Fig. 4(e). The high gain of the PD is attributed to ultrafast carrier transit time (4 ps) and long carrier lifetime (3.4–9s) as depicted in Fig. 4(f). To identify the gain mechanism, authors plotted the gain as a function of inverse of transit time shown in Fig. 4(g). The linear dependence of gain on inverse of transit time signifies the photoconductive operation of the device. Moreover, the low NEP and associated high D* enables the PD for weak light detection (Fig. 4(h)).

The broadband characteristics of graphene-based photodetectors can be further extended to THz regime by waveguiding technique as observed in a SiN waveguide integrated graphene photodetector with the split-gate configuration from the works of Muench et al. [65]. The detector has a responsivity of 12.2 V/W with 3 dB bandwidth of 42 GHz. Castilla et al. extended the range of their antenna integrated graphene p-n junction photodetector to 4.2 THz [66]. Similar results in the THz regime have been reported for 3D graphene field-effect transistors by Deng et al. [3]. Their device is responsive from UV to 3.11 THz with a moderate responsivity of 0.232 A/W. Therefore, materials engineering along with favorable device configuration can improve the PD capabilities in terms of broadband response, switching speed and weak light detection. Table 1 lists the performance parameters of the graphene-based photodetectors. Apparently, graphene-based photodetectors exhibit broadband response characteristics, ultrafast response time, and considerable responsivity and detectivity.

The optoelectronic properties of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) can be well-tuned by surface chemistry modifications, layer control, and of course the level of reduction [100]. It is an economic option for production and device fabrication as well due to its easy solution-based process ability, convenience in handling, and facile deposition techniques [101]. The photoresponsivity of rGO is a function of annealing (reduction) temperature since both its responsivity and response speed have been found to vary with process temperature [102]. The presence of oxygen functional groups greatly affects the carrier transport through rGO which to some extent can be handled by the reduction level [103]. Broadband photodetection up to THz regime has been observed in free-standing rGO [100] as well as in heterojunctions with conventional semiconductors as presented in ESI Table 1. In free-standing rGO, the content of CO functional group and O/C ratio have been found to be closely related to THz absorption and its electrical conductivity. By tuning the reduction temperature from 100 0C-350 °C, the THz responsivity have been adjusted from 10−2 to 102 mA/W [100]. Whereas, Cao et al. in their experiment recorded the photoresponse from suspended rGO annealed from 200 °C to 1000 °C and observed negative photoresponse under 1064 nm excitation in devices annealed at 800 °C and 1000 °C [102]. This switch-over in photoresponse characteristics can be exploited in binary photodetectors and in encrypted communication. In our report, we demonstrated the broadband (360–980 nm) photodetection properties of GaN/layered rGO hybrid photodetector with excellent linearity in calibration with self-powered feature [101]. The figure-of-merits are considerably improved by replacing rGO with heavily reduced graphene oxide in composition with g-C3N4 [104]. Also, we have recorded broadband optical response from heterojunctions made up of Si and rGO:CQD composite with high responsivity and detectivity [105]. Interestingly, rGO efficiently aids in the transport of photoexcited charge carriers as evidenced from our work and from the works of Yu et al. and Xu et al. Xu et al. utilized rGO as charge separation layer and carrier transport media in planar perovskite PD [106]. While, Yu et al. constructed NW core-shell p-n heterojunction with organic material core and rGO shell [107]. The organic core served as photosensitive centre and the rGO contributed to transport of carriers due to its large electrical conductivity. In a number of attempts, rGO have been utilized in heterojunction with II-VI NWs for broadband photoresponse [108,109]. Also, compound semiconductors hybrid junctions with rGO also deliver visible-to-NIR response [109].

The optical absorption spectra of CNT films cover the entire UV to Terahertz (THz) range, which makes them promising for ultra-broadband photodetection. Using a suspended CNT film with symmetric Au contacts, Liu et al. demonstrated ultra-broadband photodetection under 375 nm-118.8 μm excitation [110]. Since the absorption spectrum of individual CNTs is centered in specific visible and IR regions for interband transitions in metallic and semiconducting CNTs, the authors utilized broad distribution of tube diameters in spiderweb-like morphology to achieve broadband optical absorption. The working principle of the device is based on bolometric effect. It is an effective way to counter the strong exciton binding energy for CNT. The device responses to the variation of temperature induced by incident light and maintains a linear dependence of photocurrent with incident light intensity. Their device demonstrated UV responsivity up to 0.58 A/W and response speed of 150 μs in a vacuum. Fig. 5 (a–h) shows the power-dependent photocurrent recorded from the device under UV to THz illumination and it is interesting to see the impression of ambience on the linearity of photocurrent with incident power. The device photocurrent maintains linear relation with optical intensity in air for the long recorded range from UV to THz as featured in Fig. 5(a–d). The photoresponse in the UV spectrum is achieved due to the π plasmons where in visible to NIR region photoresponse is generated from the interband transitions of the hybrid CNTs. Under mid-IR and THz regions the photoresponse is contributed by the plasmon resonance along the main axis of the CNTs. The excited states are relaxed by releasing heat and increases the resistance of CNT. In vacuum, under similar wavelength and intensity range the devices demonstrate much higher photocurrent than that recorded under air and maintains sub-linear dependence on optical power (Fig. 5(e–h)) possibly due to lower thermal contact with air. To achieve broadband absorption, the authors used broad distribution of CNT diameters. Therefore, to have consistent broadband absorption the diameter distribution of CNTs should be under control.

Zhang et al. also used CNT ensemble for IR detection, however, exploiting the vertical photothermoelectric effect induced by CNT forest [111]. Due to the vertical alignment of nanotubes (Fig. 6 (a)), the ensemble effectively suppresses 99.4 % of reflectivity and exhibits broadband detectivity of 1.9 × 107 Jones in 2.5–25 μm excitation range. The photothermoelectric effect compels the cold p-type carriers at the bottom of CNT forest to diffuse towards the illuminated hot top side resulting in zero-bias photocurrent as shown in Fig. 6(b). The top electrode/CNT junction traps the incoming photons thereby causing thermalization of the top region of the device; while the bottom electrode works as a heat sink. This temperature gradient induces a thermoelectric voltage as shown in Fig. 6(c) under blackbody illumination. The device exhibited a peak responsivity of 640 V/W at 4.3 THz.

In a number of device configurations, single-walled CNT (SWCNT) has been utilized with other photoactive materials and carbon-based nanostructures for photodetection. SWCNTs forming Schottky junction with spatially separated graphene sheets delivered ultra-high photoresponsivity of 3000 A/W with 44 μs of response speed under 405–1064 nm wavelength. The device responsivity could be further improved by enhancing the optical absorption of SWCNT or reducing the high dark current [112]. Pyo et al. in an impressive approach to compensate for low optical absorption by CNTs interfaced porphyrin into SWCNT channel. Porphyrin assists in generating and transporting photogenerated holes to SWCNT significantly improving its responsivity to 1.6 × 10−2 A/W [113]. Spina et al. fabricated field-effect phototransistors with single CNTs sensitized with MAPbI3 perovskite [114]. Under 633 nm excitation, the device provides ultra-high responsivity of 7.7 × 105 A/W and EQE up to 1.5 × 108 %. In a different device configuration, Xu et al. incorporated SWCNTs into perovskite along with a conjugated polymer (NDI-DDP). The SWCNTs provide percolation pathways for photogenerated carriers from the perovskite and offer NIR sensitivity by forming a type II band alignment with the polymer [115]. The device presented broadband photosensitivity from 400 to 1100 nm with a peak responsivity of 400 mA/W and a specific detectivity of 6 × 1012 Jones. Bergemann and Leonard in their phototransistor used aligned CNTs sensitized with Fullerene and recorded ultrahigh negative photoresponsivity of 108 A/W in UV and visible region and 720 A/W in the NIR range [116]. Unlike conventional phototransistors whose operation is based on photogating effect, their device works depending on modulation of electron scattering and provides negative photoresponse. Table 2 lists the performance parameters of CNT based PDs integrated in different configurations and provides a comparative description of device architectures.

GQDs are nanometer-sized fragments of graphene that exhibit a bandgap due to quantum confinement suitable for a variety of optoelectronic applications. Investigations on fundamental optical properties of GQDs for device applications started over a decade ago [133,134]. Control over the energy bandgap has been attained either through size selection or surface functionalization [135]. Tetsuka et al. in their study produced nitrogen functionalized GQDs by nucleophilic substitution and dehydration reaction of amine moieties with the oxygen functional groups of GQDs [135]. With this approach, they systematically modified the effective orbital resonance of nitrogen moieties with the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of GQD. Through this surface functionalization, they tuned the HOMO-LUMO energy level difference from 0.76 eV to 2.23 eV. With these functionalized GQDs, the authors demonstrated six-fold enhancements in carrier transport in perovskite-based solar cell and ultrahigh photoresponsivity of 3.5 × 104 A/W in a hybrid phototransistor. Further, GQDs can provide ample photodetection over a broad temperature range as found from the investigations of Sehrawat and Islam [136]. The authors in their work investigated the sustainability of GQD based photon detection in a temperature range 77–623 K. They observed that in the range 93–138 K, the photodetection mechanism is controlled by carrier charging and tunneling effects while from 138 to 623 K photodetection occurs through activation energy. These qualities of tunable energy bandgap through chemical functionalization and photodetection capabilities at higher temperatures than RT make GQDs worthy for photodetection applications. Haider et al. demonstrated the UV detection mechanism of GQD-graphene hybrid PD on piezoelectric lead zirconate titanate (Pb(Zr0.2Ti0.8)O3) substrate [137]. The authors utilized the intrinsic electric field from the substrate to separate the photogenerated electron-hole pairs and to assist the transport of holes to graphene channel. They reported an ultrahigh responsivity of 4.06 × 109 A/W with 1010 gain at 325 nm excitation.

In Si process line compatible strategy, Mihalache et al. fabricated p-Si NW/GQD heterojunction-based PD for broadband detection. The thickness of GQD layer on Si NW ensemble directly impacted the rectification characteristics and photodetection performance including specific detectivity and EQE. The GQDs develop a type I band alignment with Si which suppresses the dark current from the heterojunction and the GQDs being poly(ethyleneimine) (PEI) assisted, the charge photomultiplication is determined by PEI induced electron trap levels [138]. The Si/GQD heterojunction exhibits Schottky junction like rectification. The dark current has been found to be directly related to the GQD shell thickness. Under broadband illumination the device delivers enhanced photocurrent which is also a function of GQD layer thickness. They recorded a high current on/off ratio of 0.9 × 102 with 25 A/W responsivity in the visible spectrum. In place of using a type II heterojunction that favors photoexcited carrier transport due to built-in electric field developed across heterojunction, the authors used type I band alignment to reduce the dark current and to compensate for the carrier transport they utilized functionalized GQDs to have photomultiplication. This is an effective way to approach the design of hybrid heterojunctions where there is ample freedom for surface functionalization of organic semiconductors and type II band alignment is not formed. However, inclusion of a highly conducting channel material could further improve the figure-of-merits of the GQD/Si PD and possibly evade the detrimental effects of thicker GQD shell layer.

Nguyen et al. used nitrogen-doped GQDs as photon absorber (Fig. 6(a)) in field-effect phototransistor with 2D WSe2 channel [139]. This is another example of hybrid heterojunction PD where the non-type II band alignment is fully utilized. Nitrogen-doped GQDs are efficient light absorbers and the monolayer WSe2 exhibits high mobility for efficient transport of charge carriers. Fig. 7 (a) shows the schematic of the fabricated PD where nitrogen-doped GQDs are decorated on monolayer WSe2. Monolayer WSe2 based PD does not exhibit well-defined photodetection characteristics as demonstrated in time-dependent photodetection characterization in Fig. 7(b). When integrated with nitrogen doped GQDs, the device exhibits efficient photodetection and commendable stability. The hybrid PD shows responsivity of 2578 A/W with stability of 30 days. When GQDs are brough in contact with WSe2 electrons from GQDs are transported to WSe2 which raises the Fermi level of WSe2 causing effective n-doping (Fig. 7(c)). Under optical excitation electron-hole pairs are generated in GQDs and the holes are transported to WSe2 and electrons are blocked in GQDs due to conduction band spike at the heterojunction. The trapped electrons in GQDs contribute to photogating effect and improve the PD gain. The responsivity of the device reaches to 2578 A/W when gate bias (VGS = −60 V) is applied. The photogating effect has also been exploited in 2D and 2D/3D heterojunctions; however, 0D carbon/2D TMDC heterojunctions are much more promising as higher photoabsorption is achieved in 0D, desirable surface functionalization and associated band gap tuning can be performed and upon integration with 2D TMDC, their high carrier mobility is not compromised due to van der Waals interaction. GQDs/CQDs in combination with conventional semiconductors have successfully been applied for broadband photodetection, which is listed in Table 3 along with their performance parameters.

The optoelectronic features of CQDs/GQDs are promising and their applications are widespread; however, their multifaceted research has been facing several critical shortcomings which need to be addressed attentively. One major shortcoming is that there are still no matured or standardized strategies to synthesize CQDs/GQDs with accurately defined size and size distribution, crystallinity, structure, morphology, and defects. The formation mechanism of CQDs/GQDs through bottom-up approach i.e. the reaction pathways and the nucleation mechanism are still unknown. Moreover, control over the attachment of surface functional groups CQDs/GQDs surface has still not been achieved which prevents to realize desired luminescence efficiency. Besides, due to high surface activity, CQDs/GQDs coalescence with other QDs and form larger aggregates. This is the reason why CQDs suspension does not exhibit stable size-distribution over time. Furthermore, large numbers of surface defect states detrimentally affect the luminescence efficiency. All these critical issues are to be addressed to utilize CQDs/GQDs to their full potential and desired application. In view of photodetection, heterogeneous integration of CQDs/GQDs with desired size-distribution on foreign materials is essential and their luminescence efficiency must be optimized. Therefore, research on the synthesis and nucleation of CQDs/GQDs should be emphasized.

Graphene nanoribbons (GNR) are structurally one-dimensional strips of monolayer graphene where one-dimensional band structure limit is applicable. Confinement in lateral direction occurring in GNRs can induce a bandgap whose magnitude depends on the width of the nanoribbons. The confinement induced bandgap can be approximated by the formula
Δ E
