and W is in nanometers. The ballistic carrier transport characteristics and semimetallic behavior of GNRs encouraged the scientific community to explore GNRs properties, both theoretically and experimentally. Although GNRs have been studied in the past two decades, its potential application in optoelectronics began few years ago. The distinct difference between graphene and GNRs regarding optical characteristics is that in GNRs the electronic bandgap and the optical response can be tailored with ribbon width unlike graphene which demonstrate wavelength-independent absorbance for visible light [151]. In fact, the nanoribbon morphology has been proved to be a deciding factor for photodetection performance. Denk et al. in their study illustrated the fully anisotropic optical properties of narrow GNRs dominated by excitonic effects that rely on exact atomic structure. With reflectance difference spectroscopy on N = 7 armchair GNRs, the authors demonstrated the absorption features of GNR centered at 2.1, 2.3, and 4.2 eV [152]. This intriguing feature can be well leveraged in ‘band selective’ or 'bandpass' photodetection on a single material system. Zigzag shaped GNRs has demonstrated much better photoresponse compared to armchair GNRs from the theoretical study by Ostovari and Moravvej-Farshi [153]. Interestingly, the quality of source-drain contacts can potentially influence GNR photodetection characteristics. The authors designed 12.7 nm long and 1.11 nm wide zigzag and armchair GNRs with asymmetric source and drain contacts (graphene topped with Au and Ti) and under zero bias conditions they derived that zigzag and armchair GNRs can detect photons of energies 2.72 eV and 5.02 eV respectively. This again signifies the potential of GNRs on band selective photodetection depending on its structure. However, a deterministic process to control the zigzag and armchair nature of GNRs has still not been developed. Polymer assisted nanolithography, longitudinal unzipping of CNTs and ultrasonic exfoliation from graphite are well demonstrated strategies for fabrication; however, those are not adequate for controlling alignment and assembling. Well defined alignment of GNRs is crucial for polarization sensitive optoelectronics. Alavi et al. in their study addressed this issue and demonstrated the polarization dependent photodetection characteristics in armchair GNR based photoconductive photodetector having symmetric Ti/Au electrode as drain and source [154]. The authors recorded measurable photoresponse when the polarization of the incident light is along the GNRs while for polarization perpendicular to the aligned GNRs provided lesser photocurrent as demonstrated in ESI Figure S2. The ratio of dark currents from the two orientations (along polarization of light and perpendicular to polarized light) has been estimated to be 27 which is directly dependent on external drain-source bias. The results are in agreement with armchair GNR based short channel transistors where carrier transport occurs through inter-ribbon hopping [154,155]. The observed orientation dependent drain current is due to the fact that the number of hopping events required to cross the gap perpendicular to GNRs is much more than for transport along GNRs. The authors estimated the responsivity of the PD as 0.035 mA/W for 635 nm excitation at 2 V bias. It has been argued that for GNRs aligned perpendicular to the polarization of light, the increase in photocurrent is caused due to heating from incident light. However, the photoresponsivity is considerably lower which is attributed to high contact resistance of Schottky contacts. Besides, the photoconductive design of the detector may have impeded carrier transport. Both these two issues are resolved in the work of Candini et al. where the GNR PD is designed as a field-effect transistor with multilayer graphene electrodes [156]. The authors argue that the use of graphene electrodes result efficient current injection/extraction compared to previously reported GNR based devices using metal electrodes. They also emphasize that since the drain and source contact regions are exposed to the incoming light due to the transparency of graphene, the responsivity is reasonably enhanced. The photogenerated current is found to be strongly gate dependent with highest drain current received at gate bias of −20 V. The device exhibits high photoresponsivity of 5 × 105 A/W at 550 nm excitation. This work is a typical milestone for realizing all graphene optoelectronic devices.

In spite of the striking features, a matured synthesis scheme has still not been developed that can enable deterministic assembling of GNRs and heterogeneous integration with other nanostructured materials. BCP assisted nanolithography has been demonstrated to fabricate long GNR arrays [157]; however, their alignment is greatly influenced by lamella formation which is questionable for large area coverage [158]. Besides, the suitability of transfer printing to handle GNRs for device fabrication has not been adequately investigated. Not only that, their usefulness for next generation flexible optoelectronics is still under question due to insufficient studies. It is necessary to further explore the possibilities to integrate GNRs with other nanostructures for tunable broadband photodetection and to have acute control over morphology tuning to achieve desirable optical absorption.

Widespread application of Si and Ge in electronics has led the interest of integrating NC with these semiconductors, which maintain compatibility and ensure performance with stability. In fact, it is an effective route to compensate for low optical absorption of graphene particularly. Graphene is the first NC that was used with Si for photosensing. It develops a stable Schottky junction with Si and researchers are still utilizing this heterojunction in advanced device configurations [159–162]. The matured process technology of Si and established methods for graphene synthesis/transfer together have contributed to its popularity. While Si/graphene heterojunctions find applications in visible and IR sensing, other nanostructures of carbon, like CQDs or GQDs which have comparatively better optical emission/absorption in UV when integrated with Si, exhibits high UV photoresponse and stability as well. In our report, we have demonstrated the photodetection mechanism of Si/CQD heterojunction-based PDs, where we have recorded a responsivity of 1 A/W at 360 nm [105]. The CQDs were synthesized by standard hydrothermal method and they were forming well-defined rectifying junction with p-Si. However, the responsivity achieved was only 0.1 A/W in p-Si/as prepared CQD heterojunction. The responsivity was successively improved by dispersing the CQDs in RGO and enhancing the optical absorption by Ag nanoparticles. RGO assisted in photoexcited carrier transport and Ag nanoparticles for their surface plasmon resonance in visible regime caused higher photon absorption. The responsivity in the similar heterojunction is further enhanced to 16 A/W by modifying the morphology of Si substrate surface. By decorating Au impregnated CQDs on Si NW ensemble formed by metal-assisted chemical etching, Si/Au-CQD core-shell heterojunction was realized and carrier transport was facilitated using RGO as channel [163]. Higher photoresponse is achieved as a result of higher photon trapping by the non-reflective NW ensemble through multiple internal reflections inside NW ensemble. The effective surface area in heterojunction is enhanced in core-shell configuration that renders improved carrier attraction than planar heterojunctions. In an opposite approach, to enhance the absorption of photons, Yang et al. prepared Si nanoholes (SiNH) on Si substrate and incorporated graphene into the holes conformally by low-pressure RF-PECVD technique as shown in Fig. 8 (a). Fig. 8(b) demonstrates the plan view of fabricated device [164]. The authors recorded 90 % optical absorption in the conformal nanohole configuration in the wavelength range of 400–800 nm. They intensively investigated the mechanism of high absorption by numerical modelling of the light trapping behaviors of SiNH/graphene heterojunction. The simulated reflection and absorption spectra as demonstrated in Fig. 8(c) and (d) respectively, exhibit only 10 % reflectance and 90 % absorption for SiNH/conformal graphene which agrees with experimental results. In fact, incident photons undergo a lot more reflection pathways in SiNHs than planar Si resulting better photon trapping as shown in Fig. 8(e–g). Interestingly, from the simulated electric field it is found that the highest electric field in SiNHs (1.15 V/m) is lower than that of planar Si (1.65 V/m) indicating reduced reflections in SiNHs. Moreover, the power absorption density reaches 1679 W/m3 for SiNHs while for planar Si interface, the density is only 595 W/m3 as presented in Fig. 8(f–h)The device exhibited an impressive photoresponse of 2720 A/W with a response speed of 6.2 μs at 635 nm excitation. Although the research resolves the issue of high reflectance from planar Si surface and figure-of-merits are promising, incorporating the graphene material inside SiNH is not possible by available ex-situ dry or wet transfer techniques making batch processing difficult.

The strong infrared absorption of Si also aids in realizing infrared PDs. The range of active photodetection can be further enhanced with device engineering. Liu et al. in their work took the advantage of photothermionic effect of graphene by growing graphene nanowalls in heterojunction with Si for photodetection at 1550 nm [165]. Graphene nanowalls are three-dimensional material grown by longitudinal overlap of 2D graphene and its optical absorption can reach 16 %. Graphene nanowalls were grown directly on Si for enhanced IR absorption and the barrier height between Si and graphene nanowalls was reduced by placing Au nanoparticles at their interface to promote photothermionic effect. With the plasmonic effect imposed by Au nanoparticles, the authors controlled the graphene/Si interface barrier height and eventually the hot electron movement from graphene to Si, which resulted in a response time of 370 μs. Due to strong mid-IR and THz absorption in graphene nanowalls, photoresponse at 3.5 μm and at 01–10 THz were also achieved without any complicated waveguide structures. Using similar heterojunction, Guo et al. extended the operational bandwidth of the detector to 40 GHz employing a hybrid plasmonic waveguide [166]. Broadband photodetection has also been demonstrated in the interdigitated Schottky and gated region in graphene/Si heterojunction [167]. Gate controlled responsivity and tunable spectral characteristics have also been achieved in this heterojunction by proper device architecture and interface states passivation [168,169]. Table 4 lists the performance parameters of graphene/Si–Ge heterostructure based PDs and presents a comparison in efficiency among various device configurations reported over the past decade.

The growth of III-V NWs on graphitic substrates has been undertaken over the last decade for fundamental studies on nucleation and growth for photovoltaic applications [185–188]. Especially for graphene, the growth of III-V NWs on it follows van der Waal's epitaxy other than the conventional epitaxy. In fact, integration of these two sections of materials is technologically promising due to the tunable optical properties of III-Vs and high mobility and flexibility in graphene. Out of several forms of NC, interestingly only graphene and flexible graphite has been utilized with III-arsenides, III-phosphides, and III-nitrides in different device configurations for photodetection application. Graphite is one of the most economical options for growth substrate and it embodies all necessary features for an epitaxial growth CVD substrate, e.g. high electrical and thermal conductivity, high mechanical robustness, mechanical flexibility. Due to their high electrical conductivity and optical transparency, graphitic substrates can be an economic alternative for conventional ITO. Anyebe et al. in their work have performed a thorough investigation of growing InAs NWs on graphite and have observed rectifying behavior in the I–V characteristics of InAs/graphite heterojunction. The heterojunction develops a depletion region for holes due to the differences in graphite work function (4.7 eV) and as-prepared InAs Fermi level (5.0 eV). Under excitation from 2.4 to 3.7 μm wavelength, the heterojunction provides ample photovoltage, which makes it suitable for photodetection in the NIR regime [189]. Further improvement in photoresponse can be achieved by enhancing InAs NW density to enhance optical absorption and photocurrent. Introducing a buffer layer to minimize the lattice mismatch between InAs and graphite may render better heterojunction.

Tian et al. utilized the structural benefit of a 2D/3D heterointerface between graphene and GaAs to enhance the gain of the detector by Fermi level alignment in the interface [190]. The mechanism is contrary to the conventional carrier recirculation mechanism in graphene/semiconductor heterojunction where either photoexcited electrons or holes are trapped in the semiconductor (due to energy barrier) and holes or electrons are recirculated in the graphene channel thereby prolonging the carrier lifetime and consequently the photoconductive gain. The authors utilized the Fermi level alignment effect in graphene due to graphene/GaAs interface states and simultaneously improved the carrier lifetime and reduced the carrier transit time. Under optical excitation, electron-hole pairs are generated in GaAs and under the built-in electric field the charge carriers are driven towards the heterojunction. The interface states act as hole trapping centres and the photoexcited holes are trapped in the heterointerface. These trapped holes drive the Fermi level downward which lowers the graphene Fermi level due to Fermi level alignment effect. Therefore, the graphene current improves noticeably under optical excitation and improves with increasing intensity. The authors achieved a high photoresponse of 1321 A/W at 650 nm excitation with 0 V gate voltage. The photoresponse enhances with higher back gate voltage as it improves the separation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs.

Wu et al. followed an exciting approach of using NaYF4Yb3+/Er3+nanoparticles on graphene/GaAs heterojunction to enhance its responsivity [191]. The device provided responsivity of 5.97 mA/W and a specific detectivity of 1.1 × 1011 Jones under 980 nm illumination. Miao et al. in their work fabricated a vertically stacked heterostructure of graphene and InAs NWs to compensate for the low optical absorption of graphene [192]. The authors attempted to modulate the Schottky barrier height between graphene and InAs by adding back gate potential for optimizing the photoresponse. Their device showed a high photoresponsivity of 0.5 A/W with a high on/off ratio of 500 under 457 nm–1000 nm excitation. In a recent report, Zheng et al. have investigated the UV detection performance of graphene-semiconductor-graphene back-to-back Schottky junction photodetector with InGaN nanorods as semiconductor [22]. The Schottky barrier field at both ends of InGaN nanorods efficiently extracts the photoexcited carriers and delivers ultrahigh responsivity of 105 A/W along with a fast response speed of 50 μs. The double Schottky junction with graphene has led to high switching speed of the PD. The energy band diagram in Fig. 9 (a) demonstrates two back-to-back connected Schottky diodes in series with semiconductor bar. Under UV light, electron-hole pairs are generated in the graphene/InGaN heterointerface (Fig. 9(b)). When bias is applied, one Schottky junction is forward biased and the other one is reverse biased. Due to this, the forward bias Schottky junction barrier gets lowered while the reverse biased junction gets heightened. This induces strong band bending at the heterojunction and the built-in field drives the photoexcited electrons towards forward biased junction and the holes towards reverse biased junction giving rise to measurable photocurrent as demonstrated respectively in Fig. 9(c) and (d). The device figure-of-merits are recorded under applied bias of 1 V and no self-powered characteristics were observed. However, in place of using similar Schottky contacts, by using asymmetric Schottky connections across the InGaN nanorod a built-in electric field across the nanorod can be maintained and self-powered features can be achieved.

Graphene has also shown its suitability in efficient photodetection when integrated with III-V compounds with structures other than NWs. Using InGaN colloidal QDs with graphene, Hu et al. have achieved ultrahigh photoresponsivity of 109 A/W with 109 current gain and 5.8 × 1014 Jones of detectivity at 450 nm excitation [193]. The available literature apparently shows that the graphene/III-V heterojunction-based devices hold promise for broadband photodetection and this scheme will be in a major concern for future optoelectronics. Table 5 lists the performance parameters of reported photodetectors on graphene/III-V heterostructures.

II-VI compound semiconductors in conjunction with NC have attracted great attention of the scientific community in realizing optoelectronic devices due to their direct optical bandgap and high stability in ambience. Especially ZnO NWs in combination with graphene has been used in several device configurations for UV and broadband photodetection. Also, graphene has been utilized in different device designs for optimized photoresponse. In a recent report, Huang et al. have fabricated a UV photodetector (365 nm) using ZnO nanotubes with dispersed graphene flakes on an ultra-nanocrystalline diamond substrate [200]. The device exhibited an ultrahigh UV switching ratio of 19,708 at 5 V external bias. Nanocrystalline diamonds exhibit a few unique properties like high thermal stability, electrical conductivity, high mobility, high optical bandgap, and negative electron affinity which prompted the authors to investigate its photodetection properties. The dispersed graphene flakes and nanocrystalline diamond enhances the UV absorption and imposes fast recombination of electron-hole pairs causing high on/off ratio. The low dark current from the heterojunction is attributed to chemisorbed O2 by ZnO nanotubes. When exposed to ambience, ZnO adsorbs O2 and chemisorbed oxygen species are formed at graphene-ZnO-nanocrystalline diamond interfaces. The chemisorbed species trap electrons from the conduction band thus increasing the depletion region width. Therefore, under dark condition, the conductivity through heterojunction is minimized resulting very low dark current. Under UV light, electron-hole pairs are generated and chemisorbed oxygen forms O2 molecules with migrated holes. Consequently, the depletion region width is reduced and considerable UV photocurrent is recorded. The transport of electron/hole is eased by the dispersed graphene flakes. The authors did not clear the individual impression of nanocrystalline diamond substrate; however, the strategy for achieving low dark current in ambience is indeed commendable. The sensitivity of the PD for low power measurements can be further improved by optimizing chemisorption of oxygen.

Dang et al. in their field-effect UV phototransistor used a hybrid channel composed of graphene and ZnO nanorods and recorded ultrahigh response of 3 × 105 A/W and gain of 106 [201]. The device operated in both photoconductive and photovoltaic mode due to the change in transfer characteristics under UV illumination. The working principle is similar to Ref. [200] considering the effect of chemisorbed oxygen and graphene. The Fermi level of graphene is actuated with ‘left over’ electrons after holes are migrated to compensate chemisorbed oxygen. On the other hand, Nie et al. utilized only the Schottky junction developed between graphene and ZnO nanorod for UV detection and recorded 113 A/W of responsivity [202]. The Schottky junction-based photodetector showed a fast response speed of 0.7 m s for rise time and 3.6 ms for recovery. Li et al. in their work used nanoparticles of TiO2 with tubular graphene field-effect transistor [203]. The tubular configuration certainly added a few advantages to the photodetection mechanism. The tubular structure works as an optical 3D resonant microcavity which can enhance the optical field inside it and increases the interaction area. The author demonstrated the responsivity of 475.5 A/W at 325 nm radiation.

With rapid advancements in device architectures, the performance of photodetectors observed a steep surge. In an advanced level of experimentation, Sahatiya et al. and Zhang et al. have utilized pyro-phototronic effect and piezophototronic effect, respectively for broadband photodetection [204,205]. The pyro-phototronic effect was exploited in ZnO/graphene heterojunction for self-powered NIR detection where the depletion region width is modulated by the developed pyro-potential that may add/subtract to the built-in potential; whereas, the piezophototronic effect was demonstrated in a complex structure composed of the carbon fiber core and ZnO/CdS microwire double-shell as shown in Fig. 10 (a). The schematic device structure and optical microscope image of the device is presented in Fig. 10(b) and (c) respectively. The device demonstrates considerable improvement in forward bias photocurrent with increasing intensity at 480 nm as presented in Fig. 10(d). The on-off switching characteristics in Fig. 10(e) show asymmetric rise time and fall time due to complex carrier generation and recombination mechanism. Interestingly, the PD displays large dynamic range maintaining linear dependence of photocurrent on broad range of excitation intensity (Fig. 10(f)). The device offered very high photoresponsivity of 1.11 × 105 A/W at 480 nm, 3.83 × 104 A/W at 548 nm, and 1.94 × 105 A/W at 372 nm. The responsivity of the device was further improved by 60 % with 0.38 % of compressive strain as demonstrated in ESI Figure S3. The cost-effective flexible substrate and inexpensive solvothermal synthesis are certainly advantageous for this PD implementation. Similar device structure can be attempted on textile fibers to improve a commercial option. A number of device configurations have been examined to deliver broadband photodetection which is listed in Table 6 .

Semiconducting layered transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDCs) are potential materials for electronic and optoelectronic applications because of their superior electrical and optical properties [211]. Importantly, the bandgap tunability in 2D TMDCs can be achieved through varying the number of layers that offer a lot of freedom in the optimization process [212]. Besides, the van der Waals epitaxial structure in TMDCs, i.e. absence of dangling bonds makes this class of materials highly immune to short channel effects, which aid in fabricating ultrashort channel transistors [213]. Monolayer TMDC based PDs exhibit excellent quantum efficiency. To further explore their potentialities as next-generation PDs researchers have attempted to integrate TMDCs with graphene in van der Waals heterostructure and have observed desirable improvements in performance. Tan et al. investigated the photoresponse characteristics of graphene/WS2 heterostructure in simple graphene-WS2-graphene architecture. They utilized graphene as an electrode with WS2 monolayer and bilayer in place of conventional Au electrodes [214]. The finite density of states in graphene allows the photoresponsivity to be modulated by electrostatic gating which is normally not attained in conventional metal electrodes. The authors compared the characteristics of the devices with monolayer (ML) and bilayer (BL) WS2 fabricated in the lateral configuration as shown in Fig. 11 (a and b). Under illumination power of 2.5 × 107 mW/cm2 of 532 nm the devices exhibited responsivity of 3.5 A/W for BL and 2.5 A/W for ML WS2. Increased optical absorption in thicker BL WS2 rendered higher responsivity. Fig. 11(c) demonstrates the energy band diagram of graphene/WS2 Schottky junction. Due to the difference in graphene work function and electron affinity of WS2, a Schottky barrier is developed. When positive gate bias is applied, electrons are injected into graphene which diminishes the Schottky barrier as pictorially demonstrated in Fig. 11(d). Tunability in graphene work function has already been established and the authors in their work demonstrated that for both ML and BL WS2 the Schottky barrier height decreases with increasing positive gate bias as shown in Fig. 11(f) and (g) respectively. This kind of work function tunability in metal-WS2 heterojunction is not observed due to Fermi level pinning from heterojunction interface defects. When light falls on WS2, electron-hole pairs are generated and electrons are transported to graphene causing n-doping of graphene (photogating) which further reduces the Schottky barrier rendering better photocurrent as shown in Fig. 11(e). The working mechanism remains same for other type of TMDCs.

Yeh et al. resorted to different strategy for utilizing the photogating effect. With similar WS2 monolayer grown by CVD, they fabricated a graphene-WS2-graphene hybrid heterostructure covered with In atoms as schematically demonstrated in Fig. 12 (a) and (b) [215]. Firstly, the In adatoms enhance the gain of the detector by transferring the photogenerated electrons to the WS2 channel. Secondly, it imparts the photogating effect due to trapped holes in during the recirculation of electron allowing a further increase in responsivity with applied gate bias. Fig. 12(c and d) illustrates the superiority of graphene contacts over Au electrodes. The mechanism is similar to that of [214]. The enhancement in photoresponsivity has been possible due to photogating effect induced by In adatoms as demonstrated in Fig. 12(e). Under optical excitation, large number of electron-hole pairs are generated in adatoms. Electrons are transported to WS2 while holes remain trapped in. These trapped holes act as gate and modulates the channel conductivity. The device exhibited an ultrahigh responsivity of 2.6 × 103 A/W with a response time of 40–65 μs at 550 nm excitation. This is a promising approach and the suitability of other photoactive quantum dots to impart photogating effect is worth investigating. Zhou et al. used similar graphene-BL WS2-graphene heterojunction, however, in vertical stacking configuration for photovoltaic mode photodetection [216]. In this specific vertical configuration, the authors utilized the long intralayer exciton lifetime and small intralayer exciton binding energy of BL WS2 compared to ML WS2 to optimize the photovoltaic response.

Monolayer MoS2 exhibits 104 times more luminescence quantum yield compared to bulk MoS2 and has been used broadly in realizing wide bandwidth PDs as demonstrated in Table 7 . Schottky junction made up of ML MoS2 and graphene offers ample photodetection at 1440 nm excitation with 1.26 A/W responsivity and ultralow NEP of 7.8 × 10−12 W/(Hz)1/2 as reported by Vabbina et al. [217]. Wu et al. in their recent report used similar graphene/MoS2 heterojunction along with SiNx waveguide to improve the responsivity by coupling the evanescent light with MoS2. Their device showed a responsivity of 440 mA/W at 532 nm with 80 m s and 40 m s of response and recovery time, respectively [218]. Zhang et al. in their vertical van der Waals heterostructure based visible-to-NIR PD used MoTe2 sandwiched between graphene layers [219]. The double Schottky barriers have been found to assist in photocurrent generation and transport. Wei et al. reported to the similar sandwiched structure of MoTe2 and graphene, however, they used asymmetric p-type and n-type graphene contacts with multilayered MoTe2 to create a large built-in electric field for self-powered broadband (400–1400 nm) photodetection [99]. In a totally different approach, Li et al. recently fabricated a broadband PD by sandwiching graphene in between p-type MoTe2 and n-type SnS2 [220]. MoTe2 and SnS2 exhibit complementary bandgaps with the interlayer graphene assisting efficient exciton dissociation and carrier transfer. With a multilayer graphene section, the device showed a responsivity of 2600 A/W with a specific detectivity of 1013 Jones. Other than MoS2, WS2, MoTe2, SnS2 large number of 2D TMDCs have been used in conjunction with graphene for photodetection application as listed in Table 7. The heterostructured devices are fabricated directly in the CVD technique or dry transfer method or through exfoliation with specific advantages in each case. Apart from symmetric TMDCs (MX2: M = Mo, W …; X = S, Se, Te …) discussed so far, 2D asymmetric TMDCs (MXY: M = Mo, W …; X≠YS, Se, Te ….) have been predicted to exhibit impressive optoelectronic and piezotronic properties that can be revamped using NC [221–224]. There are some hurdles in synthesizing 2D asymmetric TMDCs through chemical route which we are presently trying to resolve in our group and we firmly believe that the integration of NC with asymmetric TMDCs is going to set a new direction in future optoelectronics research especially in piezophototronic PD.

Other than transition metal dichalcogenides black phosphorous (BP) and hexagonal-boron nitride (h-BN) are emerging 2D layered materials which have a wide range of optoelectronic applications in conjunction with graphene due to their thickness-dependent semiconducting properties. As a narrow direct bandgap layered semiconductor, BP has optical properties intermediate between semimetallic graphene and wide bandgap TMDCs [245]. Due to the presence of lone pair of electrons at each phosphorous atoms, which can strongly interact with out-of-plane atoms, BP exhibits higher out-of-plane conductivity compared to TMDCs [246]. Unlike TMDCs, few layer BP exhibits direct bandgap. It is a 2D layered crystal with an orthorhombic crystal structure in which each phosphorous atom is covalently bonded to three neighboring phosphorous atoms within a plane. BP also demonstrates high carrier mobility on the order of 10,000 cm2 V−1s−1 in the bulk and 1000 cm2 V−1s−1 in flakes, much superior than TMDCs. However, BP suffers with severe degradation when exposed to ambient oxygen and moisture. Besides, BP forms large Schottky barriers with metal electrodes which prevents carrier transport. Liu et al. in their study using BP has resolved these two bottleneck situations by using graphene encapsulation on BP in a vertical heterojunction phototransistor. The authors have reported ultrahigh photoresponsivity of 3.3 × 103 A/W and photoconductive gain of 1.3 × 109 under 1550 nm illumination [247]. Fig. 13 (a) demonstrates a linear dependence of photocurrent with source-drain bias under zero gate bias. The photocurrent increases with increasing optical intensity. The photocurrent exhibits well-defined gate-dependence as shown in Fig. 13(b). Under optical excitation, electron-hole pairs are generated in BP and the holes are transported to graphene while the electrons get trapped in BP. These trapped electrons in BP further attracts more holes in graphene layer giving rise to photogating effect. That is why with increase in optical intensity (higher electron trapping in BP) the peak photocurrent shifts towards positive gate bias. The spectral response characteristics in Fig. 13(c) does not show any consistent dependence on wavelength possibly due to the interference induced by insulating SiO2 substrate. However, the photocurrent is much enhanced in BP-graphene heterojunction. More importantly, the device is highly responsive under commercially useable 1550 nm excitation as demonstrated in the temporal characteristics in Fig. 13(d). Further, the device photocurrent/responsivity demonstrates non-linear dependence on incident optical power which signifies complex electron-hole pair generation and recombination kinetics (Fig. 13(e)). With higher electron mobility in thicker BP, the photocurrent enhances dramatically for thicker samples as depicted in Fig. 13(f). Similar dependence is observed in case of photoconductive gain (Fig. 13(g)). With similar device configuration, Xu et al. have investigated the photodetection performance of graphene covered BP flakes and reported high photoresponsivity of 55.75 A/W at 655 nm, 1.82 A/W at 785 nm and 0.66 A/W at 980 nm [248]. The working principle of the photodetector is similar to Ref. [247] where the trapped electrons in BP induce photogating to graphene and improves the recombination lifetime of photocarriers. Under laboratory environment, longest stability period for BP photodetector has been recorded to be 60 days, which although promising needs to be better.

Graphene/h-BN heterojunctions are promising for the development of interband semiconductor devices like lasers, photodetectors due to the gap-less electronic band structure of graphene and control over doping at a very low level [249]. Very recent reports on the THz sensitivity of graphene/h-BN heterostructure undoubtedly unveil its possibility for efficient THz detectors. In a dual gate dipolar antenna design with a gap of 100 nm, Castilla et al. have obtained noise equivalent power of 80 pW/Hz1/2 with response time below 30 ns [66]. In the novel device design (Fig. 13(h)) with narrow gap antenna, the authors have exploited the photothermoelectric (PTE) effect due to which a p-n junction is created in the graphene channel located above the antenna and the incoming radiation is focused at the p-n junction where the photoresponse is generated. In the PTE effect, absorbed THz radiation warms up the graphene electrons and causes a heat-driven photoresponse with asymmetric device configuration. Here, the authors have utilized the asymmetry of a p-n junction for an ultrasensitive response. Whereas, Kim et al. in their vertical graphene/h-BN/Au van der Waals junction have utilized the asymmetry due to dissimilar Schottky barrier [26]. This asymmetric Schottky barrier device suppresses the dark current due to the large electron barrier developed between graphene and h-BN. Their device has shown a high on/off ratio of 225 with 7 nm thick h-BN and a fast rise time of 12 μs and a fall time of 5 μs.

The combination of perovskites with NC materials, especially graphene has been utilized for photodetection for over a few years where the perovskites served as the photoactive material and carbon provides the charge carrier pathway. Perovskites are unique semiconducting materials for photonic applications owing to their broadband optical absorption, high carrier mobilities, low exciton binding energies, and long exciton diffusion lengths [250]. Due to their appropriate direct optical bandgap in the visible regime and high absorption coefficients perovskites have been used as building blocks for efficient photodetectors in a range of optimized device configurations [251]. Surendran et al. in their work-integrated cubic lattice structured nanocrystals of CsPbBrxI3-x with graphene to investigate its photodetection capabilities and attained maximum responsivity of 1.13 × 104 A/W and specific detectivity of 1.17 × 1011 Jones at 80 μW/cm2 intensity of 525 nm excitation [252]. The device exhibited a photoconductive gain of 9.32 × 1010 due to the fast hole transit in the graphene and long recombination lifetime in CsPbBrxI3-x. The authors preferred an all-inorganic perovskite to an organic-inorganic hybrid perovskite as the later typically suffers from stability issues in humid and ambient conditions. Besides, conventionally used ammonium-based ligands maintain weak interaction with iodine anions in all-inorganic iodide-based perovskites which lead to undesired phase transformation. Therefore, to counter the stability issue the authors used an all-inorganic mixed halide CsPb(Br/I3) structure with an additional advantage of tuning the absorption spectra through (I/Br) ratio. The device has demonstrated good photocurrent stability of 95 % and 87 % respectively under continuous illumination over 5 h and periodic illumination over 37 h. However, the device experiences intensive degradation under light excitation ≥55 mW/cm2. In fact, ambient moisture is severely detrimental for 3D perovskite PDs stability and a matter of concern for device design. Shao et al. in an impressive way resolved the moisture issue and adapted a strategy for efficient electron-hole pair separation by 2D multiphase perovskites with appropriate band gaps and alignment covering monolayer graphene on h-BN [253] as shown in Fig. 14 (a). Fig. 14(b) demonstrates the energy band diagram of graphene-MAPbI3 phototransistor. Hexagonal BN flake has been used underneath graphene to maintain its high intrinsic mobility. Fig. 14(c) and (d) respectively demonstrates the schematic energy band diagram and device configuration with an energy band diagram of the heterojunction which displays the band positions of (BA)2 (MA)n-1PbnI3n+1 perovskites with different ‘n’. The positive slope in the energy band diagram can promote efficient photoexcited carrier transport. Moreover, the hydrophobic butylammonium cations are aggregated on the top of the upper surface of 2D perovskite which effectively reduces the permeation of moisture into the structure thereby improving moisture resistance and stability. Under 532 nm excitation, the device provides photoresponsivity of 105 A/W and fast response time. Besides, the 2D perovskite structure ensures a smaller density of deep hole traps compared to 3D counterparts, which aid in delivering smaller 1/f noise. This 2D multiphase perovskite structure is also promising for photovoltaic application due to effective excitonic dissociation and lesser trap assisted recombination.

Qian et al. in their hybrid phototransistor used nitrogen-doped GQDs as an effective carrier pathway and MAPbI3/rGO composite as photoactive material [254]. The solution-based processed device demonstrated broadband photoresponse in 365–940 nm range with a maximum responsivity of 1.92 × 104 A/W and 10 m s of response time. The authors extended the stability of the device to 20 days by simple PMMA encapsulation although 85 % of its initial performance was maintained. The NIR photoresponse in this device is attributed to rGO and PbI2 species. Bessonov et al. further extended the detection range of MAPbI3 based PD to 1.5 μm by embedding PbS QDs into the perovskites matrix on the graphene channel [19] as pictured in Fig. 14(e). The energy band diagram in Fig. 14(f) shows the effect of PbS absorber on the device mechanism. As PbS induces n-type doping in graphene, under illumination holes will be injected to the channel causing an increase in drain-source current when the applied gate bias is lower than VDirac. When the gate bias is higher than VDirac, the drain-source current reduces under illumination as shown in Fig. 14(f). PbS QDs in perovskites matrix not only improves surface passivation facilitating charge transport, but also produces spatially separated photocarriers, which provide large gain. Apart from ultrahigh responsivity and broadband characteristics, the self-powered operation has also been demonstrated in both rigid and flexible substrates with perovskites and graphene [255,256]. Table 8 presents a general comparison of different device architectures of perovskites/graphene heterostructure and their performance parameters.

For next-generation optoelectronic devices, fabrication, and integration on flexible substrates is going to be an indispensable criterion. Presently, it is a common concern for the scientific community in exploring the compatibility of optically active materials with flexible substrates. Fortunately, NC materials are convenient to integrate on a flexible platform, which is not normally feasible for bulk Si, Ge, III-V, II-VI, or other rigid substrates directly. The inherent flexibility in rGO allows its integration in suspended form without degrading its performance and durability [100]. In fact, the low temperature processing for rGO while tuning its optical bandgap along with its flexibility makes it promising for integration on flexible substrates like plastic and paper, which cannot withstand high temperature. Suspended graphene nanocomposites have also shown promise in realizing high-performance PDs. Yin et al. in their recent work illustrated the application of suspended PMMA assisted graphene composites for photodetection and reported extraordinary responsivity of 1 A/W in 405–1064 nm with the response speed of 100 μs [89]. Interestingly, graphene has been employed to form composites with a variety of photoactive materials (perovskites, II-VI, III-Vs, etc.) in different nanostructures (nanodots, nanowires, nanotubes, 2D, etc.) which have also exhibited their utility on a flexible platform. Dang et al. in their flexible phototransistor used MAPbI3-graphene composite as channel and demonstrated 115 A/W responsivity at 515 nm excitation with retention of device performance for over 3000 cycles of bending tests [67]. Chiang et al. in their work used graphene-GQD hybrid as the photosensitive composite on rippled PDMS supported substrate. Due to the rippled structure, their device showed commendable stretchability and strain-dependent photocurrent gain [142]. In Schottky heterojunction with ZnO NWs, graphene PDs have shown retention characteristics for more than 1000 bending cycles [208]. In a very recent report, Lee et al. used eco-friendly flexible keratin textile as a substrate for UV detection with ZnO/GQD core-shell quantum dots as photosensitive material [273]. Although the responsivity achieved from the device (68.64 μA/mW) is low, however, this environment-friendly approach can be further explored to maximize its performance. GQD based hybrid PDs have recently been realized on cotton for UV-NIR PD [274]. Other than Graphene, CNTs have been exploited in a few ways to form flexible devices with reasonable retention characteristics. Liu et al. have used CNTs with graphene, whereas Li et al. have used CNTs with perovskites and demonstrated responsivity of 51 A/W and 31.1 A/W, respectively [121,131]. The CNT network not only enhances the mechanical strength of the device but also functions as high mobility charge carrier tracks. Even after 10,000 cycles bending test the device with perovskite retained the dark current and photo gain [121]. Pyo et al. also used graphene-CNT composite on a flexible PET substrate and improved the responsivity of the device by porphyrin interfacing while retaining the transparency of the device [113]. It is apparent from the literature that all the necessary qualities of PDs like, broadband response, transparency, flexibility, self-powered operation, etc. Can be included in NC-based PDs with suitable material combinations. With further exploration, this research area will open increasing possibilities for advanced optoelectronic devices in the future. Moreover, techniques for assembling and transfer printing of 2D graphene (wet transfer, polymer stamp transfer) on flexible substrates has been established. GQDs and CQDs can be assembled on substrates at room temperature via ex-situ techniques like spray painting or spin coating. Therefore, technically there are no potential issues that may prevent the integration of graphene and CQD/GQDs on flexible platform in future optoelectronics. However, ordered assembly of CNTs and GNRs in a convenient and economic way is still an issue that needs to be addressed for commercialization.

Polarization sensitive PDs are indispensable for navigation devices, high-contrast polarizers, and polarization imaging. This kind of PDs exhibit anisotropic photoresponse to polarized light and the anisotropic dependence on polarization can be exploited as a signature of the polarization state of the detected light. The polarization sensitivity can be achieved by employing specialized features in the device like a semiconductor having anisotropic optical absorption or waveguiding techniques [275,276]. Polarization sensitive PDs using asymmetric crystal structured 2D materials (rendering anisotropic optical properties) have already been reported [277–279]; however, addition of graphene into the device configuration has improved the device performance. Dai et al. and Wu et al. in their recent studies demonstrated the importance of graphene electrodes in PdSe2 based polarization sensitive photodetectors [280,281]. The role of graphene electrodes in Refs. [280,281] are being reviewed here although it was not the central point of investigation of the authors in the respective studies. PdSe2 is a stable 2D material having puckered pentagonal structure. This anisotropic crystal structure makes it sensitive to the polarization of incident light. Dai et al. in their study used multilayered PdSe2 as the photoactive material and graphene and Au as asymmetric electrodes [280]. In this device configuration, the intrinsic optical anisotropy from PdSe2 renders polarization sensitivity; whereas, the asymmetric electrode configuration imparts self-powered characteristics through photothermoelectric effect. The polarization dependent optical absorption characteristics at 4.6 μm and 10.5 μm in polar coordinates is demonstrated in ESI Figure S4(a). The optical absorption features signify clear dependency on linear polarization. Similar linear polarization dependent characteristic feature is observed in the device generated photovoltage as demonstrated in ESI Figure S4(b) and S4(c) for 4.6 μm and 10.5 μm respectively. Interestingly, the device also exhibits self-powered characteristics due to the photothermoelectric effect from asymmetric graphene and Au electrodes. Since the thermal conductivity of graphene is much larger than that of Au, when the device is illuminated globally with IR light, the temperature of the device raises and the temperature near graphene is lower compared to that near Au due to the larger thermal conductivity of graphene. This temperature difference causes photothermoelectric effect and aids in the transport of photoexcited carriers. It is an impressive approach to achieve self-powered photodetection in a simple device configuration. Wu et al. also used graphene as the transparent conductive electrode for PdSe2 and they fabricated the device on Ge substrate (graphene/PdSe2/Ge heterojunction) as a mean to enhance the optical absorption [281]. The low optical absorption of graphene does not impede the incoming light over broadband excitation range and it also enhances photoexcited carrier collection. Besides, the narrow bandgap Ge drives the sensitivity of the PD to 1800 nm. The electric field developed across PdSe2/Ge heterojunction drives the photoexcited carriers and the anisotropic PdSe2 renders polarization dependent photocurrent. However, the polarization sensitivity of the PD has been found to be a function of excitation wavelength with decreasing sensitivity for increasing wavelength. The exact reason for this dependency has not been explored till now and requires focused research. Besides, the device configurations can be improved further to identify circularly polarized light. A number of devices involving NC are demonstrated to detect linearly polarized light for high-resolution polarization imaging and further investigations are underway to enhance the detection capability to identify circularly polarized lights [282–285].

Self-powered PDs are prioritized over conventional PDs due to their special characteristic feature of self-driving operation. It is beneficial for low power consumption and lesser hardware as there is no requirement for external power supply unit. A few strategies are already established to realize self-powered characteristics e.g. asymmetric electrode configuration, type II heterojunction with distinguishable work function difference, piezophototronic enhanced self-powered PDs each having their own merits and demerits. The asymmetric electrode configuration is comparatively a convenient approach to achieve built-in electric field strong enough to drive the photoexcited charge carriers in the absence of external bias. This configuration is applicable to both thin film and nanostructured based photoconductive PDs. Although, the electrode material generally needs to be ohmic with the photoactive material, asymmetric Schottky electrode based self-powered PDs can also be realized by using back-to-back connected Schottky contacts. However, for this case, the photoactive material should develop distinguishable Schottky junctions with both the electrode materials. Graphene having high worker function than most of the conventional bulk semiconductors is an excellent choice for Schottky contacts. Obviously, any random combination of Schottky contacts will not aid in self-powered photodetection. On the other hand, for heterojunction-based PDs, there must be significant difference in work function values of the materials forming the heterojunction. Apart from that, the heterointerface should have defects/imperfections as minimum as experimentally possible as interface defects recombine with photoexcited carriers and prevents the self-powered mechanism. Graphene and RGO in heterojunction with conventional semiconductors have demonstrated well defined self-powered feature. The same self-powered feature can be achieved in piezo-phototronic enhanced PDs where a piezoelectric field can be generated by mechanical strain and the photoexcited carriers generated from optical excitation are transported under the influence of developed piezoelectric field resulting self-powered characteristics. The piezophototronic effect is not pronounced in NCs; however, they can accelerate the carrier transfer as carrier channel material. Conventional II-VI compounds which exhibit asymmetry in their structure demonstrate well defined piezophototronic effect. However, their application for advanced self-powered devices has been decelerated since their compatibility with flexible substrates has not reached the satisfactory standards. 2D materials possess inherent compatibility with flexible substrates. For next generation advanced self-powered photodetection, 2D materials which exhibit inherent structural asymmetry will possess great promise and for wearable flexible PDs and application of graphene will continue as channel material or transparent electrode in future.

Hybrid heterostructures comprising carbon materials and semiconductors including Si/Ge, metal oxides, III-V semiconductors, perovskites, TMDCs, 2D materials, etc can be evaluated as PDs based on basic performance parameters, which solely depend on the optimized combination and usage of materials along with their morphology, device design, and configuration. The optical absorption characteristics of NC depend heavily on its morphology, which eventually determines the photosensitive wavelength range of the device. CQDs and GQDs exhibit high optical absorption in the UV region and have been used in many scientific experiments as UV PD [144,147]. Whereas, graphene, rGO, GNR, and CNTs exhibit broadband optical absorption enabling photodetection in a broad range of wavelengths (from UV to THz) [64–66]. Also, the high carrier mobility of graphene and CNT results in ultrahigh responsivity and fast switching in PDs when incorporated in proper combination [76,116]. Perovskites, which are efficiently photoactive, render ultrahigh responsivity in the range 105–107 A/W in field-effect phototransistor configuration with graphene [19,77,79]. Interestingly, the morphology of the perovskite has shown a direct impact on photoresponsivity values. Using NWs of perovskites with graphene in FET structure, the responsivity has been enhanced by one order [76,77] compared to bulk perovskite PDs. Usually, the perovskites/graphene devices exhibit sensitivity from UV to NIR range and moderate response speed of seconds or milliseconds [255,259]. While only graphene-based phototransistors in engineered configurations have provided response speed in the nanosecond range. Response and recovery speed of 265 ns and 220 ns have been recorded in 3D graphene FETs [3]. The response speed is further reduced to 24 ns in graphene p-n junction PD integrated with an antenna tuned to THz radiation [66]. Interestingly, waveguiding techniques have enabled graphene FETs to operate in THz regime with little compromise in responsivity values [65]. Besides, the other forms of NC like CQDs and C60 when integrated with graphene have shown ultrahigh responsivity over 105 A/W in the UV-NIR range [71,73,82]. Apparently, despite minimal optical absorption, graphene assists in enhancing the photoresponse considerably when used as a matrix with photoactive materials. On the other hand, rGO for its low processing temperature has been used in a variety of heterostructures for broadband photodetection [102,286]; however, the responsivity, detectivity, and response speed recorded are moderate (Ref. ESI Table 1).

CNTs likewise graphene has been investigated in combination with perovskites, C60, metal oxides, TMDCs, and have exhibited ample photoresponse around 1–100 A/W (Ref. Table 2). In a similar manner like graphene, CNTs in combination with perovskites and fullerene have shown ultrahigh responsivity of 105 A/W and 108 A/W respectively [114,116]. Moreover, when integrated with graphene, CNT PDs recorded a considerable improvement in performance compared to free-standing CNTs [112,126,127,131]. A similar trend in responsivity enhancement has been observed for GQDs. GQDs impregnated in the graphene matrix offer better photoresponse (∼109 A/W) compared to doped or functionalized GQDs [135,148].

Plasmon-enhanced graphene in heterojunction with Si exhibit ultrahigh broadband (300–1100 nm) responsivity of 1.68 × 107 A/W with 180 ns response speed [178]. While normal graphene/Si-based photoconductor offer 104 A/W responsivity and 3 μs of response speed in 632–1550 nm excitation [177]. Normally, UV-NIR responsivity is achieved in Si/carbon nanostructured heterojunction and the spectral response follows the absorption spectra of carbon materials [105,175]. Out of several conventional compound semiconductors, InGaN has been found to provide commendable responsivity and detectivity with response speed in μs scale. InGaN NWs sandwiched between graphene layers offer 105 A/W response at 380 nm excitation under 5 V bias with 47 μs and 71 μs response and recovery time respectively [22], while InGaN QDs incorporated into graphene provide 109 A/W response under low bias (1 V)at 450 nm excitation [193]. InGaN QDs provides better carrier injection under optical excitation delivering improved photodetection. Quantum dots of PbS also in accompany with graphene demonstrated 107 A/W responsivity at NIR excitation of 895 nm under further lower external bias (0.5 V) [78]. Similar performance has been observed in graphene/TMDC heterojunctions. Most importantly, MoS2 has offered visible photoresponsivity of 107 A/W at very low external bias (0.1 V), which is found to be the maximum among all TMDC/graphene heterojunctions (Ref. Table 7) [235]. Besides, GaSe and InSe in van der Waals heterostructure with graphene exhibit broadband photoresponse with responsivity comparable to commercially available Si photodiode [228,231]. ESI Table 2 lists the performance parameters of recently reported hybrid PDs which shows the indispensable application of graphene in achieving an optimum figure of merits.

Carbon materials in their various forms of structures are potentially photoactive and compatible to be integrated on semiconductor materials through standard growth methods and deposition techniques. The favorable band alignment with semiconductors with varying morphology subsequently renders effective transport of photoexcited carriers and provides a desirable figure of merits. From deep UV to THz regime, detection is feasible with moderate modifications in their device configuration and structure. The broadband optical absorption and high carrier mobility of graphene and carbon nanotubes are synergistically capable of providing a broadband response with ultrahigh responsivity and fast response time. Interestingly, all the semiconductors having bandgap in different optical regimes can be well integrated with NC consequently enhancing their spectral range of photodetection. In fact, the idea of broadband photodetection can be well employed with carbon quantum dots and graphene quantum dots in assistance with conventional semiconductors, 2D materials, and metal chalcogenides. Moreover, the extraordinary compatibility of graphene with flexible substrates paves its way for advanced optoelectronics in the future. Further research is necessary for realizing efficient van der Waals heterojunctions-based photodetectors suitable for large scale production through convenient process engineering.

Since most of the carbon nanostructures can be synthesized through facile and environment-friendly methods/techniques (except graphene and CNT due to the requirement of sophisticated vapor deposition), their potential in cost-effective processing for large scale production for a wide range of applications including PDs cannot be underestimated. Alongside facile synthesis, these nanostructures can be well integrated into devices through convenient deposition schemes like spray painting, drop coating, spin casting, etc. Therefore, the complicacy in device fabrication can be well minimized with suitably selecting the carbon materials enabling convenient processing for industrial scale. By the year 2024, the global demand for photodetectors is estimated to reach $2 billion, and due to this market demand sustainable materials and scaled up process technology is in utmost necessity. This timely need can be well met by NC-based photosensors since both the primary requirements (facile synthesis and deposition) for industrialization are satisfied. Although, the application of gold or silver as metal contacts raises some issues for its cost-effectiveness, however, with the further advancements in materials science this shortcoming of costly metal contacts will also be resolved. In fact, high conductivity MXenes materials have already been successfully applied as transparent metal electrodes for photodetectors and have performed superior to presently gold electrode-based devices. The integration of nanostructured carbon with MXenes would open a completely new interdisciplinary direction for research and future possibilities. The versatility of MXene in a variety of forms remains conductive and can be well applied with spray painting and inking process, which enables enough routes for its integration with nanostructured carbon. Further, it is expected that the stability issues with carbon photodetectors can be handled with the application of MXenes. Few metal-oxide semiconductors exhibit high photoresponsivity; however, due to their persistent photoconductivity feature they are not popular for high-speed switching applications. Metal oxides can be prepared through cost-effective facile chemical route and their suitability in high-speed optoelectronics will ensure an economic and commercial option. The nanostructured carbon, which exhibits high mobility like graphene, carbon nanotubes, graphene nanoribbons, can be experimented to optimize the carrier transport and switching characteristics of metal oxides.

Each nanostructured form of carbon has its own distinguishing features. The high electron mobility of graphene is congenial for ultrafast switching characteristics. CNTs and GNRs also facilitates fast carrier transport. However, the low optical absorption in graphene compromises its responsivity. Whereas, CNTs and GNRs exhibit measurable optical bandgap that results better optical absorption characteristics. Despite this feature, graphene is more sought after than any other carbon nanostructures for optoelectronics due to its structural compatibility with all other nanostructures. In fact, graphene provides maximum freedom in heterogeneous integration which is not possible for CNTs and GNRs as their alignment and wall morphology dictates their optoelectronic properties. Desired alignment of CNTs and GNRs is still hard to achieve; whereas, synthesis and processing of graphene has reached a matured stage. The issue of its low optical absorption can effectively be resolved by integrating graphene with high optical absorbance materials. In case graphene heterojunction-based PDs undergoes slow switching response due to indispensable defects, the same can be improved in phototransistor configuration utilizing the photogating effect. Moreover, the phototransistor configuration enables some control over the carrier transit time and carrier lifetime (if recombination is prevented) that allows a direct control over the gain of the PD. When it comes to spectral response, heterostructuring as well as waveguiding can broaden the spectral features towards FIR.

For the newly emerging augmented reality and artificial intelligence (AI) technology, the associated optical sensors are required to be transparent to form an optical interface between human eye and outside world. Besides, the color sensors utilized in AI and the internet-of-things platform must be capable of spectral discrimination with a precisely tunable spectral response. The characteristic bandgap of carbon quantum dots and the suitability of carbon materials in tuning their optical bandgap can effectively be exploited in reaching the above-mentioned conditions. In the coming days, multispectral photoresponse can be achieved by controlling their size-dependent photoresponsivity, and their intrinsic broadband absorption spectra will serve as a detection platform.

Krishnendu Sarkar: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Praveen Kumar: Supervision, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.
